title: docs: fix broken logo/favicon
description: PR #759 review: logo and favicon are broken in deployed docs. Check mkdocs.yml logo/favicon paths and ensure assets are committed.

title: Audit update_attributes.md flowchart + code links
description: Verify docs-mkdocs/climate-data-interface/processors/update_attributes.md against climakitae/new_core/processors/update_attributes.py: trace every flowchart step to source, click every code link, confirm line numbers, validate decision branches and error paths, sanity-check parameters table and examples.
close_reason: Rewrote update_attributes.md. Original described fabricated user-facing 'attrs' and 'var_attrs' parameters that do not exist — the processor's value parameter is unused (default UNSET). Real behavior: priority-9998 finalizer that merges context['new_attrs'] (populated by other processors' update_context methods) onto result.attrs and fills CF-compliant dim attrs from common_attrs. Added flowchart with click links to execute@79 and update_context@123, and clarified this processor is added implicitly by ClimateData. mkdocs build clean.

title: Audit filter_unadjusted_models.md flowchart + code links
description: Verify docs-mkdocs/climate-data-interface/processors/filter_unadjusted_models.md against climakitae/new_core/processors/filter_unadjusted_models.py: trace every flowchart step to source, click every code link, confirm line numbers, validate decision branches and error paths, sanity-check parameters table and examples.
close_reason: Augmented filter_unadjusted_models.md. Original was mostly accurate but lacked a flowchart and code-reference table. Added Mermaid flowchart with click links to execute@65, _contains_unadjusted_models@129, _remove_unadjusted_models@173. Clarified that membership test uses 'intake_esm_attrs:activity_id/source_id/member_id' joined as model_id and matched against NON_WRF_BA_MODELS, and that a single matching Dataset returns None while containers preserve type. mkdocs build clean.

title: Audit metric_calc.md flowchart + code links
description: Verify docs-mkdocs/climate-data-interface/processors/metric_calc.md against climakitae/new_core/processors/metric_calc.py: trace every flowchart step to source, click every code link, confirm line numbers, validate decision branches and error paths, sanity-check parameters table and examples.
close_reason: Rewrote metric_calc.md from scratch. Original doc had pervasive fabrications: (1) used config key 'one_in_x_config' but real key is 'one_in_x'; (2) listed metrics 'hdd_cdd', 'heat_index', 'effective_temp', 'noaa_heat_index' that live in climakitae.tools.indices not metric_calc — real supported metrics are min/max/mean/median/sum; (3) 'period' was documented as 'yearly'/'monthly'/'seasonal' strings but real format is (int, str) tuple with units 'year' or 'month'; (4) 'event_duration' was string but real default is (1,'day'); (5) 'block_size' default was 365 but real default is 1; (6) 'goodness_of_fit_test' was 'ks' but real is bool; (7) all 22+ click line numbers (L375, L390, L415, L440, L455, L470, L540, L555, L565, L585, L590, L597, L640, L655, L680, L683, L690, L705, L730, L750) were fabricated. Documented all 16 real methods with correct line ranges from __init__@145 through update_context@1387. mkdocs build clean.

title: Audit bias_adjust_model_to_station.md flowchart + code links
description: Verify docs-mkdocs/climate-data-interface/processors/bias_adjust_model_to_station.md against climakitae/new_core/processors/bias_adjust_model_to_station.py: trace every flowchart step to source, click every code link, confirm line numbers, validate decision branches and error paths, sanity-check parameters table and examples.
close_reason: Rewrote bias_adjust_model_to_station.md. Original flowchart cited line numbers (85, 95, 105, 115, 120, 130, 145) that all predate the actual source — the first method (__init__) starts at line 148. Documented real method line numbers: __init__@148, _preprocess_hadisd@185, _load_station_data@243, _bias_correct_model_data@309, _process_single_dataset@510, _execute_dict@723, execute@790, update_context@864. Expanded params from {stations} to full dict (stations, historical_slice, window, nquantiles, group, kind). Removed mention of early WRF guard that does not exist. mkdocs build clean.

title: Audit drop_leap_days.md flowchart + code links
description: Verify docs-mkdocs/climate-data-interface/processors/drop_leap_days.md against climakitae/new_core/processors/drop_leap_days.py: trace every flowchart step to source, click every code link, confirm line numbers, validate decision branches and error paths, sanity-check parameters table and examples.
close_reason: Rewrote drop_leap_days.md. Original claimed 'no configuration needed' with {} example; that call would raise AttributeError because __init__ calls value.lower(). Real param is single string 'yes' (default) or 'no'. Added flowchart with execute@63 + _process_data@113 + _drop_leap_days@178 + update_context@154. mkdocs build clean.

title: Audit convert_to_local_time.md flowchart + code links
description: Verify docs-mkdocs/climate-data-interface/processors/convert_to_local_time.md against climakitae/new_core/processors/convert_to_local_time.py: trace every flowchart step to source, click every code link, confirm line numbers, validate decision branches and error paths, sanity-check parameters table and examples.
close_reason: Rewrote convert_to_local_time.md. Original doc was substantially wrong: claimed 'timezone' (str/dict) and 'lon_based' (bool) parameters that do not exist. Real parameters are 'convert' ('yes'/'no') and 'reindex_time_axis' ('yes'/'no') from the value dict. Old example would have silently been a no-op. Documented real algorithm: catalog-routed dispatch (hdp -> _convert_to_local_time_hdp@269, else -> _convert_to_local_time_gridded@212 which requires hourly frequency), _find_timezone_and_convert@320 uses timezonefinder + pandas tz_convert, optional DST reindex. Added click links and Code References. mkdocs build clean.

title: Audit warming_level.md flowchart + code links
description: Verify docs-mkdocs/climate-data-interface/processors/warming_level.md against climakitae/new_core/processors/warming_level.py: trace every flowchart step to source, click every code link, confirm line numbers, validate decision branches and error paths, sanity-check parameters table and examples.
close_reason: Major rewrite. Old flowchart was missing real algorithm steps (Feb 29 drop, time->time_delta swap, expand_dims warming_level), referenced a non-existent _transform_gwl method (lines 200-230 fabrication), and had every line number wrong. Source actually: __init__ 73-111 (eager CSV load at L91/L94), execute 113-273 (nested key/wl loops at 165/173), update_context 308-329, set_data_accessor 331-341, reformat_member_ids 343-374, get_center_years 376-521. Rewrote Mermaid flowchart to add: reformat_member_ids, extend_time_domain, get_center_years lookup, NaN-year guard, _determine_is_complete_wl check, drop Feb 29, time->time_delta swap, expand_dims, sim_centered_years context store. Fixed Implementation Details: lookup tuple is (activity_id, member_id, source_id) not (model, scenario, ref_period); window math is asymmetric (center+window-1); missing-WL behavior is NaN+skip not None. Noted self.name = 'warming_level_simple' (context key).

title: Audit processor flowcharts and code links for accuracy
description: Line-by-line review of all 12 processor reference pages. For each page verify: (1) Mermaid flowchart steps map exactly to source-code branches in climakitae/new_core/processors/<name>.py; (2) every clickable Mermaid box link points to the correct file and a still-valid line range; (3) decision diamonds match real if/else/try-except branches; (4) error/edge-case paths are represented; (5) parameter tables and worked examples match current API. Tracked under PR #759.
close_reason: All 12 processor docs audited and corrected. Each doc now has a flowchart with verified click links to source line numbers, an accurate parameter table (eliminating fabricated keys/types/defaults), and a Code References table mapping methods to GitHub line ranges. Pervasive issues found and fixed across the audit: fabricated parameters (convert_to_local_time's timezone/lon_based, update_attributes's attrs/var_attrs, drop_leap_days's {} example), wrong config-key names (metric_calc's one_in_x vs one_in_x_config), wrong defaults (metric_calc block_size 365 vs real 1, event_duration string vs (1,'day') tuple, period 'yearly' vs (1,'year')), wrong supported metric list (metric_calc claimed hdd_cdd/heat_index — those are in tools.indices not this processor), wrong algorithms (clip's expanding-radius fabrication vs real 3x3 index neighborhood, warming_level's _transform_gwl fabrication, export's GeoTIFF fabrication), and dozens of fabricated line numbers across every doc. mkdocs build remains clean throughout.

Full rewrite of the climakitae documentation. Replace Sphinx + ReadTheDocs with MkDocs Material hosted on GitHub Pages. Center the new_core fluent API; treat legacy core/ side-by-side with migration callouts on each legacy page. Audience: climate scientists/analysts, Python integrators, and internal contributors. No fixed deadline — prioritize quality.

Out of scope: JupyterLite (climakitae's native deps aren't Pyodide-compatible).

title: Phase 3: Integrate InfrastructureLayers into DataCatalog
description:
Wire the InfrastructureLayers class into the DataCatalog singleton. Three files to modify:

climakitae/core/paths.py:
- Add EIA860M_CA_PLANTS_URL, GEM_CA_PLANTS_URL, HIFLD_CA_TRANSMISSION_URL, HIFLD_CA_SUBSTATIONS_URL, OSM_CA_POWER_URL constants
- Add INFRASTRUCTURE_LAYER_URLS: Dict[str, str] convenience dict mapping layer keys to URLs
- Add # TODO(scraping): automate GEM URL by scraping globalenergymonitor.org/projects/ comment beside GEM constant

climakitae/new_core/data_access/data_access.py:
- In __init__: add self._infrastructure = UNSET and self._infrastructure_lock = threading.Lock()
- Add @property infrastructure -> InfrastructureLayers with double-checked lock (mirror .boundaries property exactly)
- Import InfrastructureLayers from infrastructure_layers and INFRASTRUCTURE_LAYER_URLS from paths

climakitae/core/constants.py (optional):
- Add EIA_FUEL_TYPE_MAP dict if shared between ETL script and _process_eia_plants

After parquets are uploaded to S3 (s3://cadcat/infrastructure/), smoke-test with: DataCatalog().infrastructure.eia_plants

Recommended model: Haiku 4.5 — all three changes are direct pattern-matches to existing code. URL constants follow paths.py conventions exactly; DataCatalog property mirrors .boundaries line-for-line. No novel reasoning required.
acceptance_criteria: DataCatalog().infrastructure returns InfrastructureLayers instance. Property uses double-checked lock pattern matching .boundaries. uv run python -m pytest -n auto -m 'not advanced' stays green after changes. Manual notebook smoke test: DataCatalog().infrastructure.eia_plants returns valid GeoDataFrame after S3 upload.

title: Phase 2: Implement InfrastructureLayers class
description:
Create climakitae/new_core/data_access/infrastructure_layers.py mirroring the Boundaries class pattern. Update __init__.py to export InfrastructureLayers.

Class signature: InfrastructureLayers(layer_urls: Dict[str, str])

Lazy properties (double-underscore backing stores, load via gpd.read_parquet on first access, RuntimeError on failure):
- .eia_plants -> gpd.GeoDataFrame
- .gem_plants -> gpd.GeoDataFrame
- .transmission -> gpd.GeoDataFrame
- .substations -> gpd.GeoDataFrame
- .osm_power -> gpd.GeoDataFrame

Process methods (called at property load time):
- _process_eia_plants(df): sort by name
- _process_gem_plants(df): sort by name
- _process_transmission(df): sort by voltage_kv descending
- _process_substations(df): sort by name
- _process_osm_power(df): pass-through (no-op for now)

Lookup methods (all cached in _lookup_cache: Dict[str, Any]):
- _get_plants_by_name() -> Dict[str, int]: name→row index, EIA+GEM combined
- _get_plants_by_fuel_type() -> Dict[str, List[int]]: fuel_type→list of row indices
- _get_plants_by_operator() -> Dict[str, List[int]]: operator→list of row indices

Public API:
- layers_dict() -> Dict[str, gpd.GeoDataFrame]: returns already-lazy-loaded GeoDataFrames
- available_layers() -> List[str]: URL keys present in layer_urls
- preload_all(), clear_cache(), get_memory_usage() -> Dict[str, Union[int, str]], validate_urls()

All public methods require NumPy-style docstrings.

Unit tests in tests/new_core/data_access/test_infrastructure_layers.py:
- Mock gpd.read_parquet at import location
- Follow testing-elegant workflow: one test method -> run -> commit -> repeat
- Cover: lazy loading (data not loaded until property accessed), cache invalidation, lookup method correctness, memory tracking, validate_urls raises on missing URLs

Recommended model: Sonnet 4.6 — boundary between templated and subtle. Lookup-cache invariants (multi-value dicts for fuel/operator) and strict adherence to Boundaries property pattern require careful reasoning. Haiku 4.5 acceptable only for process methods and boilerplate properties after the first two are written as templates.
acceptance_criteria: Class in infrastructure_layers.py follows Boundaries pattern with correct double-underscore backing stores. All 5 lazy properties, all lookup methods, and all public API methods implemented with NumPy docstrings. Unit tests written one method at a time with commits after each passing test, achieving 80%+ coverage on infrastructure_layers.py.

title: Phase 1: Build ETL script for CA infrastructure parquets
description:
Create scripts/build_infrastructure_parquets.py — standalone CLI tool that fetches CA power plant and grid infrastructure from 5 public sources and writes 5 GeoParquet files (EPSG:4326, snappy compression). Accepts --output-dir and --gem-dir args.

Fetch functions:
- fetch_eia860m_ca(): EIA-860M monthly Excel filtered to CA; Point geometry; apply EIA_FUEL_TYPE_MAP (SUN→Solar, NG→NaturalGas, WAT→Hydro, NUC→Nuclear, BIT→Coal, DFO→Oil, GEO→Geothermal, WND→Wind, OTH→Other)
- fetch_gem_ca(gem_dir): GEM coal/gas/solar/wind tracker Excels; hardcoded March 2026 URLs + --gem-dir flag for local files; # TODO(scraping): automate via globalenergymonitor.org/projects/ comments at each URL
- fetch_hifld_transmission_ca(): ArcGIS REST Electric_Power_Transmission_Lines; paginated with resultOffset loop; LineString geometry; CA bbox filter
- fetch_hifld_substations_ca(): ArcGIS REST Electric_Substations; paginated; Point geometry (centroid if Polygon); CA bbox filter
- fetch_osm_ca(): dispatches to _fetch_osm_power_type(power_type, bbox, retries=3) for each type in [plant, generator, substation, line, cable]; exponential backoff (sleep 2**attempt); all five results concatenated into one GeoDataFrame

Standard schemas:
- Plants (EIA, GEM, OSM plant/generator): plant_id, name, fuel_type, capacity_mw, status, operator, county, source, geometry (Point)
- Transmission: line_id, voltage_kv, status, owner, source, geometry (LineString)
- Substations: substation_id, name, voltage_kv, status, owner, source, geometry (Point)
- OSM combined: osm_id, name, power_type, fuel_type, capacity_mw, voltage_kv, operator, source='OSM', geometry

Outputs: eia860m_ca_plants.parquet, gem_ca_plants.parquet, hifld_ca_transmission.parquet, hifld_ca_substations.parquet, osm_ca_power.parquet

Recommended model: Sonnet 4.6 for ETL scaffolding, OSM retry logic, and schema reconciliation. Haiku 4.5 for boilerplate fetch functions (fetch_hifld_transmission_ca, fetch_hifld_substations_ca) once the fetch_eia860m_ca template exists. Escalate to Opus 4.7 if GEM Excel schema differs significantly from documentation or Overpass returns malformed responses.
acceptance_criteria: python scripts/build_infrastructure_parquets.py --output-dir /tmp/infra produces 5 valid GeoParquet files. Each file: gpd.read_parquet succeeds, CRS is EPSG:4326, schema matches documented columns, geometry column is correct type. --gem-dir flag accepted and used when provided.
notes:
Starting implementation on feature/power-infrastructure-layers
Phase 1 done: scripts/build_infrastructure_parquets.py committed (9dbefde2). Starting Phase 2.

title: Epic: CA Power Plant & Infrastructure Data Layers
description:
Build a new InfrastructureLayers class in climakitae/new_core/data_access/ that lazy-loads CA power plant and grid infrastructure data from GeoParquet files (EIA-860M, HIFLD transmission/substations, Global Energy Monitor, OpenStreetMap). Mirrors the Boundaries class pattern (lazy-loaded properties, lookup caching, double-checked-lock singleton on DataCatalog) but exposed as a .infrastructure property since the data is point-like, not polygon boundaries.

Two deliverables:
1. ETL script (scripts/build_infrastructure_parquets.py) — fetches from 5 public sources, writes 5 GeoParquet files
2. InfrastructureLayers class + DataCatalog integration

Sources and licenses: EIA-860M (public domain), HIFLD via ArcGIS REST (public domain), Global Energy Monitor March 2026 release (CC BY 4.0), OpenStreetMap via Overpass API (ODbL). All California-only, EPSG:4326.

Recommended model: Sonnet 4.6 overall; delegate Phase 3 and boilerplate fetch functions to Haiku 4.5; escalate to Opus 4.7 for GEM schema mismatches or Overpass parsing blockers.

## Context
Design decisions: California only; direct URL constants in paths.py (not intake catalog); GEM March 2026 URLs hardcoded + --gem-dir CLI flag + TODO(scraping) comments; OSM split into one Overpass sub-query per power type (plant, generator, substation, line, cable) each with 3-attempt exponential backoff; layers_dict() returns actual GeoDataFrames (already lazy). Pattern templates: boundaries.py (class), data_access.py HadISD loading (URL pattern).
acceptance_criteria:
1. Five GeoParquet files produced: eia860m_ca_plants, gem_ca_plants, hifld_ca_transmission, hifld_ca_substations, osm_ca_power — all EPSG:4326, snappy-compressed
2. InfrastructureLayers follows Boundaries pattern (lazy properties, lookup caching, preload_all, clear_cache, get_memory_usage)
3. DataCatalog().infrastructure property works with double-checked lock
4. Unit tests in tests/new_core/data_access/test_infrastructure_layers.py with mocked gpd.read_parquet
5. uv run python -m pytest -n auto -m 'not advanced' stays green

title: Create API reference: climakitae.new_core.processors utilities (processor_utils, abc_data_processor helpers)
Deep review: export processor documentation (detailed analysis)
description:
Comprehensive review of export processor documentation against actual implementation.

CURRENT STATE:
- Flowchart is overly simplified
- Documentation doesn't capture the three distinct data structure handling modes
- Missing critical details about format selection and S3/local behavior
- No explanation of export_method branching logic

ACTUAL FUNCTIONALITY - THREE DISTINCT DATA HANDLING MODES:

1. GRIDDED DATASETS (Single xr.Dataset/xr.DataArray with lat/lon dimensions)
   - Input: Dataset with multiple lat/lon coordinate values (e.g., shape (time, lat, lon))
   - Output: Single file containing all gridded data
   - Behavior:
     * 'separated' parameter is SILENTLY IGNORED
     * 'location_based_naming' parameter is SILENTLY IGNORED
     * Both parameters apply ONLY to multi-point collections, not to gridded data
   - User Impact: Users may expect separated output but get single file instead
   - Export Formats: NetCDF, Zarr, CSV, GeoTIFF
   - Key Point: This is the PRIMARY use case for gridded climate models

2. MULTI-POINT CLIP RESULTS (Single Dataset with 'closest_cell' dimension)
   - Input: Output from clip processor with multiple points (has 'closest_cell' dim)
   - Data Structure: Single Dataset but with multiple point locations along one dimension
   - Behavior when separated=False (DEFAULT):
     * All points exported to single file
     * 'closest_cell' dimension preserved
     * location_based_naming ignored
   - Behavior when separated=True:
     * Dataset split along 'closest_cell' dimension
     * Each point gets separate file
     * If location_based_naming=True: Filenames include lat/lon (e.g., myfile_34-05N_118-25W.nc)
     * If location_based_naming=False: Filenames include index (e.g., myfile_0.nc, myfile_1.nc)
   - Export Formats: NetCDF, Zarr, CSV, GeoTIFF
   - Key Point: Users need clear guidance on when to use separated=True

3. POINT COLLECTION (List/Tuple of xr.Dataset/xr.DataArray objects)
   - Input: List of single-point datasets (output from cava_data with separate_files=True)
   - Data Structure: Each item has scalar lat/lon coordinates (size 1)
   - Behavior when separated=False (DEFAULT):
     * Each item exported normally with unique filenames
     * Automatically appends _0, _1, _2 etc. to avoid conflicts
     * location_based_naming ignored
   - Behavior when separated=True:
     * Each point gets dedicated file
     * If location_based_naming=True: Includes lat/lon in filename
     * If location_based_naming=False: Uses index-based naming
   - Special Case: cava_data concatenated output (separate_files=False)
     * Points concatenated along 'simulation' dimension into single Dataset
     * Treated as gridded data (cases 1 above)
     * separated/location_based_naming ignored
   - Export Formats: NetCDF, Zarr, CSV, GeoTIFF
   - Key Point: Separate_files workflow vs batch workflow produces different results

FOUR FORMAT EXPORT MODES:

1. NETCDF (DEFAULT)
   - Engine: xarray netcdf4
   - Compression: zlib enabled
   - Chunking: Auto-chunked
   - Cloud Support: No (local only)
   - Method: data.to_netcdf(filename, engine='netcdf4', encoding={...})
   - Best For: Long-term archival, CF conventions, widespread compatibility
   - File Extension: .nc

2. ZARR (CLOUD-OPTIMIZED)
   - Engine: xarray zarr with rasterio backends
   - Chunking: Zarr chunking with consolidation
   - Local Storage: Saves to local filesystem as directory structure
   - Cloud Support: S3 via mode='s3' parameter
   - Method: data.to_zarr(store=path, mode='w', consolidated=True)
   - Best For: Distributed access, large datasets, cloud workflows
   - File Extension: .zarr (directory, not single file)
   - S3 Behavior: Uploads to S3 bucket with s3:// protocol
   - Key Point: S3 export ONLY works with Zarr format

3. CSV (TABULAR)
   - Engine: xarray to_csv via pandas
   - Compression: gzip compression applied
   - Structure: Flattens coordinates to columns
   - Cloud Support: No (local only)
   - Method: da.to_dataframe().to_csv(filename + '.gz')
   - Best For: Spreadsheet analysis, data sharing, small datasets
   - File Extension: .csv.gz (gzip compressed)
   - Limitation: Only works well for 1D/2D data

4. GEOTIFF (RASTER)
   - Engine: rioxarray with rasterio
   - Format: GeoTIFF with geospatial metadata
   - Projection: Preserves CRS and spatial info
   - Cloud Support: Limited (some S3 support via GDAL)
   - Method: data_array.rio.to_raster(filename)
   - Best For: GIS software import, single raster layers, spatial analysis
   - File Extension: .tif or .tiff
   - Limitation: Works best with 2D data (lat, lon only)

FILENAME GENERATION STRATEGY:

1. Basic Filename
   - Uses 'filename' parameter (default: 'dataexport')
   - Adds format-specific extension (.nc, .zarr, .csv.gz, .tif)

2. Location-Based Naming (for separated multi-point export)
   - Format: {filename}_{lat}-{lon}.ext
   - Example: climdata_34-05N_118-25W.nc
   - Decimal conversion: 34.5 → 34-5 (dashes replace dots to avoid extension confusion)
   - Hemisphere encoding: Positive lat → N, Negative lat → S, Positive lon → E, Negative lon → W
   - Example: -118.25 becomes 118-25W

3. Index-Based Naming (alternative for separated multi-point export)
   - Format: {filename}_{index}.ext
   - Example: climdata_0.nc, climdata_1.nc, climdata_2.nc

4. Custom Filename Template
   - Parameter: filename_template
   - Supports placeholders: {filename}, {lat}, {lon}, {name}
   - Example: '{name}_data_{lat}N_{lon}W'
   - Lat/lon only populated for single-point data

5. Duplicate Handling
   - If file exists: Appends _1, _2, _3 (unless export_method='skip_existing')
   - Example: climdata.nc → climdata_1.nc → climdata_2.nc
   - Skip Existing: Returns None if file already exists (useful for re-runs)

EXPORT METHOD CONTROL:

1. export_method='data' (DEFAULT)
   - Exports data and returns (dataset, filepath)
   - Allows downstream processing after export

2. export_method='raw'
   - Only exports raw/unprocessed data
   - Uses raw_filename parameter (default: '{filename}_raw')

3. export_method='calculate' or 'calculated'
   - Only exports calculated/processed data
   - Uses calc_filename parameter (default: '{filename}_calc')

4. export_method='both'
   - Exports both raw and calculated data
   - Creates two separate files
   - Uses raw_filename and calc_filename parameters

5. export_method='skip_existing'
   - Skips export if file already exists
   - Returns None instead of data
   - Useful for pipeline re-runs

6. export_method='none'
   - Skips export entirely
   - Returns input data unmodified
   - Useful for dry-runs or testing

CRITICAL MISSING DOCUMENTATION:

1. Data Structure Complexity
   - Current docs don't clearly explain the 3 handling modes
   - Users confused about when separated=True applies
   - No flowchart showing how input type determines behavior

2. S3/Cloud Behavior
   - Only Zarr supports S3 export
   - Other formats silently ignore mode='s3' and export locally
   - No error warning if user tries NetCDF + s3

3. Format Selection Logic
   - Auto-detection from extension not documented
   - Case-insensitive handling not documented
   - Format validation not shown

4. Separated Output Behavior
   - No clear examples showing dict vs list handling
   - No explanation of 'closest_cell' dimension splitting
   - No mention of duplicate filename handling

5. Performance Characteristics
   - No discussion of memory usage
   - No guidance on optimal chunk sizes for Zarr
   - No notes on export speed for different formats

6. Data Preservation
   - What metadata is preserved per format?
   - How are attributes handled?
   - What about encoding information?

FLOWCHART ISSUES:

1. Input type routing oversimplified
   - Doesn't show the 3 distinct data structure detection
   - No branching based on 'closest_cell' dimension
   - Missing logic to detect list vs single dataset

2. Export method branching incomplete
   - Shows format selection but not export_method logic
   - No representation of raw/calculated/both mode
   - Missing skip_existing and none options

3. S3 vs local decision tree missing
   - No check for format compatibility with S3
   - No warning generation for invalid combinations
   - Missing fallback behavior

4. Line number accuracy
   - Referenced lines need verification
   - Many click directives likely outdated

DOCUMENTATION GAPS:

1. Examples section
   - Missing: separated multi-point with location_based_naming
   - Missing: raw/calculated data separation example
   - Missing: custom filename template example
   - Missing: Zarr S3 export with proper authentication notes
   - Missing: error handling (duplicate files, invalid formats)

2. Parameters table incomplete
   - No detailed explanation of export_method values
   - Missing constraints on format/mode combinations
   - No warnings about ignored parameters (separated for gridded)

3. Implementation Notes section missing
   - No discussion of format-specific behaviors
   - No notes on when each format is appropriate
   - No performance guidance

4. Troubleshooting section missing
   - No guidance for common errors
   - No explanation of S3 credential requirements
   - No notes on file size limitations

REVIEW CHECKLIST:

- [ ] Flowchart clearly shows three data handling modes (gridded/closest_cell/collection)
- [ ] Input type detection logic documented
- [ ] 'separated' behavior clearly explained for each mode
- [ ] location_based_naming format documented with examples
- [ ] export_method branching fully represented (data/raw/calculate/both/skip_existing/none)
- [ ] S3 vs local behavior explained with warnings for invalid combinations
- [ ] Format comparison table accurate and complete
- [ ] Examples cover gridded, multi-point, and collection exports
- [ ] Example shows location_based_naming with correct lat/lon format
- [ ] Example shows raw/calculated separation
- [ ] Example shows custom filename template
- [ ] S3 authentication requirements documented
- [ ] Duplicate filename handling explained
- [ ] Performance characteristics discussed
- [ ] Metadata preservation per format explained
- [ ] Line number references verified
- [ ] Flowchart uses proper Mermaid click directives (not HTML)
- [ ] Code complexity matches documentation detail
close_reason: Implemented comprehensive Export documentation with three data handling modes, format-specific details, and silently ignored parameters. Commit 5553681f

title: Deep review: clip processor documentation (detailed analysis)
description:
Comprehensive review of clip processor documentation against actual implementation.

CURRENT STATE:
- Flowchart appears reasonable but lacks critical detail
- Documentation shows basic input types but missing smart point-handling logic
- Line number references need verification

ACTUAL FUNCTIONALITY - FOUR DISTINCT INPUT MODES:

1. BOUNDARY CLIPPING (Named Boundaries)
   - Input: String (e.g., 'Los Angeles', 'CA', 'San Francisco Bay Watershed')
   - Source: Loaded from DataCatalog boundaries (geopandas GeoDataFrames)
   - Multi-boundary union: ['Alameda', 'Contra Costa'] merged via .dissolve()
   - Separation mode: Returns dict with boundary names as keys
   - Process: Load → Validate → Union/Keep separate → Clip via rioxarray masking

2. COORDINATE CLIPPING (Bounding Boxes)
   - Input: Tuple ((lat_min, lat_max), (lon_min, lon_max))
   - Creates bbox geometry via shapely.geometry.box()
   - Process: Create box → Validate → Clip via rioxarray masking
   - No separation logic (single box = single result)

3. POINT CLIPPING (Single & Multiple Points) ⭐ COMPLEX LOGIC
   - Input: Single tuple (lat, lon) OR list [(lat1,lon1), (lat2,lon2), ...]
   - SMART NEAREST-CELL SEARCH:
     * First attempts to get closest gridcell via get_closest_gridcell()
     * If closest cell is NaN, searches in expanding radii: 0.01°, 0.05°, 0.1°, 0.2°, 0.5°
     * Returns closest valid (non-NaN) data point
   - Multi-point: Uses get_closest_gridcells() with vectorized operations
   - Output: Single point → scalar lat/lon; Multi-point → new 'closest_cell' dimension
   - Separated output: Dict with point coordinates as keys OR list of datasets
   - Duplicate removal: If multiple input points map to same gridcell, filters duplicates

4. STATION CLIPPING (Weather Stations)
   - Input: String station code (e.g., 'KSAC', 'KSFO')
   - Validation: is_station_identifier() checks if string is valid station code
   - Source: Loaded from HadISD station catalog (via DataCatalog)
   - Process: Parse code → Load station coords → Call point clipping logic
   - Result: Closest gridcell to station location

FILE CLIPPING (Shapefiles/GeoJSON)
   - Input: String file path (e.g., '/path/to/boundary.shp')
   - Loading: geopandas.read_file() with auto-CRS detection
   - Process: Load → Validate geometry → Reproject if needed → Clip via rioxarray

CRITICAL MISSING DOCUMENTATION:

1. Smart point-finding algorithm
   - Current docs don't explain expanding-radius search for valid data
   - This is scientifically important for WRF data (has grid cells with NaNs)
   - Users need to understand what 'closest gridcell' means operationally

2. Coordinate system handling
   - No mention of WGS84 enforcement
   - No explanation of rioxarray spatial indexing
   - No notes on projection assumptions

3. Separation mode details
   - Dict vs list output not clearly documented
   - No explanation of dict key structure for boundaries/points
   - Missing: what happens with duplicate point inputs

4. Multi-dimensional masking
   - Docs show 'Create 3D mask' but don't explain broadcasting
   - How does masking work across time, sim, and lat/lon dimensions?
   - What about data with irregular dimensions (WRF grid)?

5. Geometry validation
   - Empty geometry handling
   - Invalid CRS handling
   - Geometry simplification for rasterization

FLOWCHART ISSUES:

1. Oversimplified routing logic
   - Doesn't show station detection branch
   - Doesn't show file vs boundary vs coordinate parsing
   - Missing smart-search logic for points

2. Line number accuracy
   - Referenced lines need verification against current code
   - Many line numbers in click directives likely outdated

3. Missing sub-processes
   - No representation of nearest-neighbor search
   - No shown handling of NaN gridcells
   - No duplicate-removal step

4. Context metadata
   - What exactly gets recorded in context?
   - Boundary names? Clip extent? Search radius info?

DOCUMENTATION GAPS:

1. Examples section
   - Missing: multi-boundary separated output example
   - Missing: multi-point with location_based_naming demo
   - Missing: station clipping example
   - Missing: custom shapefile example
   - Current 'Multiple Points (Separated)' example lacks actual output structure

2. Parameters table
   - Missing explanation of how 'value' type determines behavior
   - No mention of mutually-exclusive configurations
   - No warning about 'separated' behavior with dicts vs lists

3. Performance notes
   - No discussion of computational cost for large gridded datasets
   - No guidance on when to use clipping vs other spatial ops
   - No warning about memory usage for multi-point extractions

REVIEW CHECKLIST:
- [ ] Flowchart shows all four input modes (boundary/coordinate/point/station/file)
- [ ] Smart point-finding algorithm is documented with scientific context
- [ ] Coordinate system handling explained (WGS84, projections, rioxarray)
- [ ] Separation mode behavior clearly defined for all input types
- [ ] Multi-dimensional masking strategy explained
- [ ] Geometry validation and edge cases documented
- [ ] Context metadata recording explained
- [ ] Examples cover all four input modes + separated + location_based_naming
- [ ] Line number references verified and accurate
- [ ] Flowchart uses proper Mermaid click directives (not HTML)
- [ ] Code complexity matches documentation detail level
close_reason: Implemented comprehensive Clip documentation with smart point-finding algorithm, five input modes, and multi-input handling. Commit 5553681f

title: Deep review: metric_calc processor documentation
description:
Review metric_calc processor documentation and flowchart against actual implementation.

CRITICAL ISSUES IDENTIFIED:
- Current flowchart is oversimplified and doesn't match actual code complexity
- Missing branches for three different calculation modes:
  1. Basic metrics (min, max, mean, median, sum) + percentiles
  2. 1-in-X return value analysis (extreme value statistics)
  3. Threshold exceedance counting

ONE-IN-X PROCESS (most scientifically important):
- Supports return_periods or return_values (mutually exclusive)
- Distribution fitting: GEV, Gamma, GenPareto
- Extremes type: max or min
- Block maxima extraction with configurable block size
- Goodness of fit testing (Kolmogorov-Smirnov)
- Duration filtering for consecutive exceedances
- Multi-variable support with variable-name prefixing
- Memory management for Dask arrays (small/medium/large)
- Variable preprocessing capabilities

FLOWCHART NEEDS:
- Top-level branching on calculation type
- Separate sub-flows for each mode
- For 1-in-X: block maxima extraction → distribution fitting → return value calculation
- For thresholds: consecutive filtering → resample & count
- For metrics: quantile calculation + metric selection

DOCUMENTATION IMPACT:
- Examples section shows only basic metric_calc usage
- No examples for 1-in-X return value analysis
- No explanation of the three distinct operation modes
- Links in flowchart still use HTML tags (need Mermaid click directives)

REVIEW CHECKLIST:
- [ ] Flowchart accurately represents all three calculation modes
- [ ] 1-in-X process is explained with scientific context
- [ ] Parameter documentation matches actual code defaults
- [ ] Examples demonstrate all three calculation types
- [ ] Links use proper Mermaid click directives
- [ ] Supported metrics table includes all actual supported metrics
- [ ] Code reference links point to correct line numbers
close_reason: Implemented comprehensive MetricCalc documentation with three calculation modes, flowchart updates, and code references. Commit 5553681f
